title: Fundamentals of AI and ML
objectives: Understand IAM identities (users, groups, roles, policies) and apply least‑privilege access
Describe the AWS shared responsibility model and security best practices
Design secure VPC architectures with subnets, security groups and network ACLs
Use AWS services (KMS, ACM, Secrets Manager) to encrypt data at rest and in transit
Implement multi‑account strategies (AWS Organizations, Control Tower, SCPs) and cross‑account access
description: This lesson covers the foundational concepts of artificial intelligence and machine learning that underpin the AIF-C01 exam. You will learn to distinguish AI, ML, and deep learning, and to classify problems by learning paradigm. The lesson walks through the ML lifecycle from raw data to deployed model, covering preprocessing, feature engineering, training, and evaluation. Key evaluation metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUC-ROC) are introduced alongside the concepts of overfitting and underfitting. The lesson closes with guidance on recognizing when ML is the right approach versus simpler rule-based solutions.

resources.title: AWS Solutions Architect – Associate Exam Guide (PDF)
AWS Machine Learning Foundations (AWS Skill Builder)

projectSummary: Create an encrypted S3 bucket and design an IAM policy that grants read access to a specific role.  Enable MFA‑delete and configure a CloudFront distribution in front of your bucket.

title: Fundamentals of Generative AI
objectives: Explain microservices vs. monolithic architectures and event‑driven design
Use managed services (API Gateway, SQS, SNS) to decouple components
Implement horizontal and vertical scaling strategies and auto‑scaling
Design high availability with multi‑AZ and multi‑Region architectures
Explain disaster recovery strategies (pilot light, warm standby, active‑active)
description: In this lesson you will learn how to design architectures that remain available and responsive under varying loads and failure scenarios.  Topics include microservices and serverless patterns, message queues, caching and CDN acceleration.  You will explore horizontal versus vertical scaling, auto‑scaling groups, load balancing and failover strategies.  The lesson also introduces disaster recovery approaches such as backup‑and‑restore, pilot light, warm standby and active‑active.  You will evaluate when to use containers (ECS/EKS) versus serverless (Lambda/Fargate) and how to integrate multiple AWS services to achieve loose coupling and resiliency.

resources.title: AWS Solutions Architect – Associate Exam Guide (PDF)

projectSummary: Use the Amazon Bedrock Playground to compare zero-shot, few-shot, and chain-of-thought prompts against a foundation model of your choice. Document how response quality changes across strategies. Then configure a Knowledge Base (RAG) backed by an S3 document and contrast grounded vs. ungrounded responses.

title: Foundation Model Applications with Amazon Bedrock
objectives: Select the appropriate storage service (S3, EFS, EBS) based on performance needs
Choose compute options (EC2, Fargate, Lambda, Batch) for elasticity and performance
Design high‑performance database solutions (Aurora, DynamoDB, caching)
Create scalable network topologies and choose load‑balancing strategies
Use auto‑scaling and monitoring to meet performance objectives
description: Amazon Bedrock is the primary AWS service for building generative AI applications and the most heavily tested service on AIF-C01. This lesson covers the Bedrock model catalog (Anthropic Claude, Amazon Titan, Meta Llama, Mistral, Cohere) and how to select a model based on capability, latency, cost, and context-window requirements. Bedrock Agents are introduced as the orchestration layer that lets foundation models call external APIs and query knowledge bases. Model customization options are compared: continued pre-training, supervised fine-tuning, and RAG. The lesson also covers Bedrock Guardrails for content filtering, PII detection, and topic blocking, and explains how to evaluate model quality using automated metrics and the model-as-judge pattern.

resources.title: AWS Solutions Architect – Associate Exam Guide (PDF)

projectSummary: Deploy a web application on EC2 behind an Application Load Balancer with an Auto‑Scaling Group.  Integrate an Aurora Serverless or DynamoDB database and add an ElastiCache layer to cache read‑heavy queries.

title: Guidelines for Responsible AI
objectives: Use AWS cost‑management tools (Cost Explorer, Budgets, Cost and Usage Reports)
Select cost‑effective storage classes and lifecycle policies
Choose appropriate compute purchasing options (On‑Demand, Reserved, Savings Plans, Spot)
Design cost‑optimized database and network solutions
Implement tagging and multi‑account strategies for cost allocation
description: The final lesson covers designing architectures that meet business requirements while minimizing cost.  You will learn how to use AWS Cost Explorer, Budgets and cost allocation tags to monitor and control spending.  Storage topics include S3 storage classes, Intelligent‑Tiering, data lifecycle rules and hybrid storage options.  Compute topics cover pricing models such as On‑Demand, Spot Instances, Reserved Instances and Savings Plans.  The lesson also addresses cost‑effective database choices and network design, including NAT gateways versus instances, data transfer costs and content delivery strategies.

resources.title: AWS Solutions Architect – Associate Exam Guide (PDF)

projectSummary: Use Amazon SageMaker Clarify to generate a bias report on a sample dataset. Identify at least two bias metrics (e.g., Class Imbalance, Disparate Impact), interpret what each means in context, and propose a mitigation strategy. Write a brief Model Card for a hypothetical model using AWS's Model Card template.

title: Security, Compliance, and Governance for AI
objectives: Apply AWS IAM least-privilege principles to AI services (Bedrock, SageMaker, Comprehend)
Understand data encryption requirements for AI workloads: at rest (KMS) and in transit (TLS)
Describe VPC isolation patterns for AI inference endpoints
Explain AWS compliance frameworks relevant to AI (GDPR, HIPAA eligibility, SOC 2)
Use AWS CloudTrail and Amazon CloudWatch to audit and monitor AI service usage
description: The security and governance domain (~14%) applies familiar AWS security patterns to AI-specific contexts. This lesson covers IAM policies for Bedrock and SageMaker: who can invoke models, who can create knowledge bases, and how cross-account access is controlled. Data protection requirements are reviewed: KMS encryption for training data in S3 and SageMaker feature stores, TLS for API calls to Bedrock. VPC endpoint configurations that keep inference traffic off the public internet are introduced. The lesson also covers compliance frameworks AWS customers must understand when deploying AI in regulated industries, and how CloudTrail model invocation logging and CloudWatch anomaly detection support ongoing governance.

resources.title: AWS Certified AI Practitioner Exam Guide (PDF)

projectSummary: Create an IAM role with least-privilege permissions for Bedrock InvokeModel (scoped to one model ARN). Enable CloudTrail logging for Bedrock API calls, invoke the model, and verify the call appears in CloudTrail. Configure a VPC endpoint for Bedrock and confirm model invocations route through it.
